windows.title: App Store Gold Rush
windows.mechanism: iPhone + App Store created a new distribution channel with zero incumbents and willing consumer payers. First-mover discovery advantage; App Store search worked. CAC was organic.
effects: Early utility/game developers extracted years of zero-CAC distribution
Created the template for every subsequent 'new channel' window
Discovery centralization (the App Store algorithm) was both the open and the close signal
windows.decay_logic: Discovery centralization made it pay-to-play by 2012-13; CAC inflation; categories crowded

entry_checklist.text: Was it pre-2013?
Did you have organic discovery before the algorithm required paid?

who_died: Every category entrant after 2013 with no distribution edge

future_paths.path: No future paths — closed window
future_paths.falsifier: N/A — already closed

windows.body: iPhone + App Store created a new distribution channel with zero incumbents and willing consumer payers. First-mover discovery advantage; App Store search worked. CAC was organic.

windows.title: ZIRP SaaS Cycle
windows.mechanism: Zero interest rates + cloud infrastructure made SaaS unit economics look better than they were. 10× ARR multiples rewarded growth at any cost. Capital was free; sales + marketing was the only constraint.
effects: Cohort of 100× ARR companies built during ZIRP that can't survive rate normalization
SaaS playbook (land-and-expand, net revenue retention focus) institutionalized
Rate shock 2022 repriced the 2021 vintage violently
windows.decay_logic: Rate shock (Fed 2022+) repriced duration; growth-at-any-cost became burn-rate scrutiny

entry_checklist.text: Did you have negative churn (NRR >100%)?
Could you raise at 20× ARR to fund growth that was negative FCF?

who_won: 2015-2019 vintage SaaS companies that grew into their multiples; Snowflake/Datadog/Crowdstrike
who_died: 2021 vintage at 100× ARR that never grew into valuation; most SPACs

future_paths.path: No future paths — closed window
future_paths.falsifier: N/A — rate normalization confirmed close

windows.body: Zero interest rates + cloud infrastructure made SaaS unit economics look better than they were. 10× ARR multiples rewarded growth at any cost. Capital was free; sales + marketing was the only constraint.

windows.title: AI Wrapper / Capability Diffusion Window
windows.mechanism: GPT-4-class APIs commoditized → solo developers could build consumer-grade AI products → short-video organic distribution (TikTok/IG/FB creators) gave zero-CAC reach → existing willingness-to-pay (diet, productivity, education) already proven.
effects: Cal AI: $2k marketing test → $30M ARR → MyFitnessPal acquisition (8-9 figures)
Base44: solo, zero funding → $1M ARR in 3 weeks → Wix $80M acquisition → $150M ARR post-acquisition
Acqui-hire wave: incumbents buying winners rather than building (Windsurf→Google $2.4B, Scale→Meta $14.3B 49%)
windows.decay_logic: Token price deflation ~100x+ compresses margins toward cost floor; platform absorption (labs ship the feature); incumbents acquire the winners; CAC inflation as the channel matures; distribution advantage erodes as every competitor uses same playbook

entry_checklist.text: Is there a new capability API that incumbents haven't yet absorbed into their products?
Is there a distribution channel (TikTok/IG/Discord/LinkedIn) where organic reach is still free in your category?
Does existing willingness-to-pay exist (people already pay for this without AI)?
Can you get to first revenue in <2 weeks with <$10k?

who_died: Thousands of 2023-24 thin wrappers erased by platform feature releases; CAC inflation; model-price deflation
windows.open_signal: Solo apps still hitting $1M+ ARR in weeks (Base44 did in Feb 2025). When that stops — when first-month revenue requires VC and a team — the column flips to CLOSED.

future_paths.path: Window stays open through 2026 as new capability layers (multimodal, agents) create new wedges
future_paths.falsifier: No solo-founder app reaches $1M ARR in <30 days after 2026

future_paths.path: Window closes by end 2026 as platform absorption accelerates
future_paths.falsifier: New solo-founder $30M ARR app documented in H1 2027

future_paths.path: Window fully closed by 2027; all remaining alpha is institutional
future_paths.falsifier: Sustained solo-founder exits >$50M after 2027

windows.body: GPT-4-class APIs commoditized → solo developers could build consumer-grade AI products → short-video organic distribution (TikTok/IG/FB creators) gave zero-CAC reach → existing willingness-to-pay (diet, productivity, education) already proven.

windows.title: Acqui-hire Wave 2024-25
windows.mechanism: As the capability window closes for independents, incumbents buy talent and time rather than building. The clearest evidence: valuation disconnected from revenue (Windsurf ~$2.4B on modest ARR). Mechanism is defensive: the talent/product would have competed with them.
effects: Inflection → MSFT ~$650M (Mar 2024)
Character.AI → Google ~$2.7B license (Aug 2024)
Adept → Amazon
Windsurf → Google ~$2.4B (Jul 2025, after OpenAI's $3B fell through)
Scale AI → Meta $14.3B for 49% (Jun 2025) [cf:md — verify]
windows.decay_logic: Window closes when: (1) incumbents have absorbed enough talent, (2) regulatory scrutiny makes acquisitions prohibitive, or (3) no remaining independent targets worth the price

entry_checklist.text: Does your product/team address a capability gap an incumbent needs in <18mo?
Are you building in a space where MSFT/Google/Meta/Amazon would face antitrust risk building internally?
Is your team irreplaceable enough to justify acquisition vs hiring away?

who_died: Every well-funded AI startup that raised at peak and failed to become an acqui-hire target
windows.open_signal: CLOSING. The wave of major acqui-hires may be ending as regulatory scrutiny increases (FTC/DOJ watching Microsoft-OpenAI structure).

future_paths.path: Regulatory pressure closes acqui-hire arbitrage; talent consolidates at incumbents
future_paths.falsifier: Major acqui-hire ($500M+) after 2026 without regulatory challenge

future_paths.path: Acqui-hire wave continues as new capability gaps emerge
future_paths.falsifier: 3+ major acqui-hires in 2026-27

windows.body: As the capability window closes for independents, incumbents buy talent and time rather than building. The clearest evidence: valuation disconnected from revenue (Windsurf ~$2.4B on modest ARR). Mechanism is defensive: the talent/product would have competed with them.

windows.title: Agentic Vertical Services (SMB Ops)
windows.mechanism: Tool-use reliability crossed service-quality thresholds in 2025. Unsexy SMB verticals — voice receptionists, AI bookkeeping, compliance automation, intake systems — have existing willingness-to-pay (they already pay humans for this) and distribution wedges that don't require paid ads (referral networks, vertical software partnerships).
effects: Dental/medical practice management: replacing receptionist + scheduling
Restaurant/service business: order taking + reservations
Bookkeeping/accounting: small business compliance automation
Legal intake: initial client screening and document collection
windows.decay_logic: Platform bundling (Quickbooks/ServiceTitan add the feature); per-seat price collapse as models commoditize; CAC inflation as category gets competitive

entry_checklist.text: Is there a regulated/painful phone-and-paper workflow in this vertical?
Does existing human-service spend prove willingness-to-pay (not speculative)?
Is there a distribution wedge that doesn't require paid ads (referral network, vertical software integration)?
Can you serve 80% of the workflow without 100% reliability (the last 20% still goes to human)?

who_won: Fieldwork (HVAC service), Dentistry.AI, early vertical agents
who_died: Generalist AI receptionists without vertical depth; companies that tried to build horizontal and hit CAC walls
windows.open_signal: Agent-powered SMB services still growing 3x+ YoY in unsexy verticals. When growth requires VC-scale CAC to acquire the next SMB, the window is closing.

future_paths.path: Window stays open 2025-2028; vertical depth wins over horizontal reach
future_paths.falsifier: No new vertical agent company reaches $10M ARR without VC-scale spend by end 2027

future_paths.path: Platform bundling closes window by 2027
future_paths.falsifier: Quickbooks/ServiceTitan/HubSpot fail to add native agent features by 2027

windows.body: Tool-use reliability crossed service-quality thresholds in 2025. Unsexy SMB verticals — voice receptionists, AI bookkeeping, compliance automation, intake systems — have existing willingness-to-pay (they already pay humans for this) and distribution wedges that don't require paid ads (referral networks, vertical software partnerships).

windows.title: Energy / Grid Software + Behind-the-Meter
windows.mechanism: The AI power constraint IS the market. Interconnection queues (5yr median), sold-out turbines, 2-4yr transformer lead times create demand for: (1) optimization software that extracts more value from existing capacity, (2) behind-the-meter solutions that bypass the queue entirely, (3) demand-response automation at DC scale. The constraint is the moat.
effects: Behind-the-meter gas generation (Abilene model) becomes standard for hyperscaler DC builds
Virtual power plants (VPPs) and demand-response software become critical infrastructure
Interconnection queue optimization becomes a multi-hundred-million dollar market
Transformer supply optimization: 2-4yr lead times create premium for software that reduces transformer count
windows.decay_logic: Window closes when: interconnection queues clear (unlikely before 2030), or when hardware shortage resolves, or when AI capex growth plateaus

entry_checklist.text: Does your software attack a constraint node (queue, transformer, turbine) rather than a nice-to-have?
Can you demonstrate ROI in weeks vs the 5-year queue alternative?
Do you have a relationship with a utility or hyperscaler as anchor customer?

who_died: Pure-play software companies without hardware integration in a market that requires both
windows.open_signal: PJM capacity auction prices still elevated (was 10× recent norms). When prices normalize AND queues clear, the premium shrinks.

future_paths.path: Energy software + BTM stays as major category through 2030
future_paths.falsifier: US grid interconnection queue median drops below 2yr by 2028

future_paths.path: Nuclear restarts + new transmission resolves the constraint by 2028
future_paths.falsifier: Palisades + 3 new SMR sites operational by 2028

windows.body: The AI power constraint IS the market. Interconnection queues (5yr median), sold-out turbines, 2-4yr transformer lead times create demand for: (1) optimization software that extracts more value from existing capacity, (2) behind-the-meter solutions that bypass the queue entirely, (3) demand-response automation at DC scale. The constraint is the moat.

windows.title: Robotics Embodiment / Physical AI
windows.mechanism: LLM-powered reasoning is now available; the bottleneck has moved to physical actuation + sim-to-real transfer. When cheap actuators (Boston Dynamics, 1X, Figure) cross the reliability threshold AND sim-to-real data generation solves the training problem, the window opens. Pre-ChatGPT-moment for physical AI.
effects: Warehouse + fulfillment automation (Amazon, Foxconn deployment)
Last-mile delivery automation
Construction + manufacturing automation in structured environments
Elder care + service robotics (demographic tailwind: Japan/Korea aging)
windows.decay_logic: Window may never fully open if sim-to-real gap proves too hard; OR opens and closes quickly as incumbents (Amazon Robotics, Fanuc) absorb the capability

entry_checklist.text: Is actuator reliability above 99.9% for the specific motion required?
Does sim-to-real transfer work for your target environment (structured > unstructured)?
Is there a data flywheel (each deployment generates training data for the next)?
Does the unit economics work WITHOUT robotic uptime improving (i.e., human-comparable today)?

who_died: Rethink Robotics (Rodney Brooks, too early 2012-18); early warehouse robot companies without software moats
windows.open_signal: FORMING. Signal to watch: first mass-deployment (1000+ units) of a general-purpose humanoid in a single vertical.

future_paths.path: General-purpose humanoid reaches 1000-unit deployment in one vertical by 2028
future_paths.falsifier: No mass humanoid deployment in single vertical by end 2028

future_paths.path: Physical AI remains specialized/narrow; no general-purpose window opens
future_paths.falsifier: General humanoid economic parity with human labor by 2030

future_paths.path: China leads embodied AI due to manufacturing proximity + data advantage
future_paths.falsifier: Western humanoid company leads on deployment metrics by 2028

windows.body: LLM-powered reasoning is now available; the bottleneck has moved to physical actuation + sim-to-real transfer. When cheap actuators (Boston Dynamics, 1X, Figure) cross the reliability threshold AND sim-to-real data generation solves the training problem, the window opens. Pre-ChatGPT-moment for physical AI.

windows.title: Post-Starship Orbital Economics
windows.mechanism: Full Starship reuse (both booster + ship) would collapse $/kg to LEO by ~10-50×, making orbital infrastructure economically viable for compute, manufacturing, and potentially point-to-point transport. Gated entirely on Starship achieving commercial full-reuse cadence.
effects: 1M-satellite constellation becomes economically buildable (SpaceX/xAI FCC filing)
In-orbit manufacturing of materials impossible on Earth (perfect crystals, alloys)
Point-to-point hypersonic transport (SpaceX original thesis)
Orbital solar power (long-horizon, energy constraint response)
windows.decay_logic: Window may not open at all if Starship full reuse proves technically infeasible at the required cadence. Or opens and is dominated by SpaceX exclusively (single-player market).

entry_checklist.text: Has Starship achieved >5 consecutive full-reuse flights at commercial cadence?
Is $/kg to LEO below $200 sustainably (from current ~$1,500-2,000)?
Does your compute/manufacturing use case have thermal management solutions for orbit?
Is your business model independent of a single launch provider (SpaceX monopoly risk)?

who_won: TBD — pre-window; SpaceX is both gate-keeper and likely first beneficiary
who_died: Every orbital compute startup before full Starship reuse — capital consumed before the window opened
windows.open_signal: FORMING. Signal: Starship full-reuse reflown at commercial cadence (target: 5+ consecutive reflight successes).

future_paths.path: Starship full reuse achieved → orbital economics viable → window opens
future_paths.falsifier: No reflown Starship at commercial cadence by end 2028

future_paths.path: Thermal/launch-cost math fails; orbital compute remains economically infeasible
future_paths.falsifier: Third-party analysis confirms orbital compute economics by 2030

future_paths.path: Window opens but SpaceX captures it exclusively; no independent entry possible
future_paths.falsifier: Non-SpaceX orbital infrastructure company raises $1B+ in 2028-30

windows.body: Full Starship reuse (both booster + ship) would collapse $/kg to LEO by ~10-50×, making orbital infrastructure economically viable for compute, manufacturing, and potentially point-to-point transport. Gated entirely on Starship achieving commercial full-reuse cadence.

windows.title: Individual Leverage Inflection
windows.mechanism: As AI compresses execution cost, scarcity migrates to distribution + judgment + speed. Windows get shorter and more frequent. Individual leverage rises — solo $1M-ARR-in-weeks now exists (Base44 documented). The 'one-person $1B company' is the stated frontier claim.
effects: Solo-founder $30M ARR (Cal AI) and $150M ARR (Base44 post-acq) now documented
Windows get shorter — Base44 was 3 weeks from launch to $1M ARR
Distribution + taste become scarcer than engineering
VC-backed teams compete against zero-overhead solos in capability windows
windows.decay_logic: Weakens if the next window's winner-list is dominated by incumbents only — that would signal individual-leverage thesis has broken down. Alternatively: platform absorption makes first-mover distribution irrelevant.

entry_checklist.text: Can you build and ship a working product alone in <2 weeks?
Do you have an existing audience (LinkedIn/X/YouTube/Discord) before building?
Is your distribution edge replicable by a 10-person team with VC money (if yes, you lose)?

who_died: VC-backed 20-person teams competing in the same window as solos; lost on speed
windows.open_signal: OPEN. Still winning: new solo-founder documented exits >$50M in 2025-2026. When this stops, column flips.

future_paths.path: Individual leverage thesis holds; solo $1B ARR company documented by 2030
future_paths.falsifier: No solo-founder $100M+ ARR company documented by 2028

future_paths.path: Platform absorption makes individual speed advantage irrelevant by 2028
future_paths.falsifier: Solo-founder $100M ARR company documented after 2027

future_paths.path: Thesis holds but window narrowing; last solo-founder exit >$100M is in 2026
future_paths.falsifier: Major solo-founder exit documented in 2028+

windows.body: As AI compresses execution cost, scarcity migrates to distribution + judgment + speed. Windows get shorter and more frequent. Individual leverage rises — solo $1M-ARR-in-weeks now exists (Base44 documented). The 'one-person $1B company' is the stated frontier claim.

windows.title: AI Memory Supercycle
windows.mechanism: HBM demand from AI accelerators has structurally broken the consumer-driven DRAM cycle. AI capex → GPU demand → HBM demand → SK Hynix/Micron pricing power. The constraint cleared GPUs only to reveal advanced memory as the next binding node. Hyperscaler capex floors the demand.
effects: SK Hynix HBM sold out through 2026; ASP inflecting
Micron HBM volume ramping; new structural demand floor
DRAM pricing no longer dominated by consumer electronics cycle
New memory architectures (Processing-in-Memory, CXL) becoming relevant
windows.decay_logic: Closes when: (1) HBM capacity expansion catches demand (2027+), (2) compute architecture shifts away from memory-bandwidth-bound models, (3) AI capex growth plateaus

entry_checklist.text: Is SK Hynix HBM still on allocation (sold out)?
Is hyperscaler capex guidance still growing (>$500B/yr total)?
Are new memory architectures (CXL, PIM) still 18+ months from commercial deployment?

who_died: DRAM spot traders who didn't model the AI-capex floor; memory startups building for consumer cycle
windows.open_signal: OPEN. SK Hynix HBM on allocation. When allocation ends + spot price declines — window closing.

future_paths.path: Memory supercycle extends through 2027 as AI capex stays elevated
future_paths.falsifier: HBM no longer on allocation by end 2026

future_paths.path: Memory cycle peaks 2026 H2 as capacity expansion arrives
future_paths.falsifier: SK Hynix HBM ASP stable or rising into 2027

future_paths.path: New architecture (PIM/CXL) displaces HBM premium before 2028
future_paths.falsifier: HBM remains >50% of AI memory spending in 2028

windows.body: HBM demand from AI accelerators has structurally broken the consumer-driven DRAM cycle. AI capex → GPU demand → HBM demand → SK Hynix/Micron pricing power. The constraint cleared GPUs only to reveal advanced memory as the next binding node. Hyperscaler capex floors the demand.

windows.title: Nuclear Restart / Firm Power
windows.mechanism: AI datacenter load (4.4% of US electricity heading to 7-12% by 2028) collides with a grid that takes 5+ years to interconnect anything. Gas turbines sold out to ~2029. The only dispatchable, carbon-acceptable, large-block answer at hyperscaler scale is nuclear — so the buyers with infinite money (Microsoft, Google, Amazon, Meta) started signing 20-year PPAs and the politics flipped in one cycle.
effects: Three Mile Island restart (Microsoft 20yr PPA); Palisades first-ever US recommissioning (fuel loaded Oct 2025)
Constellation rerated from utility to AI-infrastructure stock
SMR pipeline (Oklo, X-energy, TerraPower) funded by hyperscaler offtakes rather than government grants — demand-pull at last
Uranium supply chain (enrichment esp.) re-emerging as a chokepoint; Russia still ~40%+ of global enrichment
windows.decay_logic: Closes via saturation (every restartable reactor restarted, ~6-8 units in the US) and SMR delivery reality-check ~2030; if AI capex cracks first, power demand growth re-rates down and the PPAs become stranded-buyer problems

entry_checklist.text: Does the play own dispatchable megawatts, restart-adjacent assets, or enrichment capacity (not just nuclear narrative)?
Is there a signed hyperscaler offtake or is it spec?
Does the timeline survive a 2-year permitting slip?

who_won: Early Oklo/SMR backers (paper gains, delivery pending)
who_died: NuScale's first SMR project (UAMPS, cancelled 2023 on cost — too early, wrong customer: municipalities not hyperscalers)
windows.open_signal: Hyperscalers still signing nuclear PPAs at premium $/MWh; interconnection queues still 5yr; gas turbines still sold out. Watch: first SMR concrete pour with a hyperscaler anchor.

future_paths.path: Every viable US restart executed + first SMR fleet orders by hyperscalers
future_paths.falsifier: No SMR order book >2GW with anchor customers by end 2028

future_paths.path: AI capex shakeout strands the power build-out; PPAs renegotiated down
future_paths.falsifier: Datacenter load growth sustains >15%/yr through 2027

future_paths.path: Enrichment becomes the binding chokepoint and re-prices violently
future_paths.falsifier: Western enrichment capacity additions announced >10M SWU by 2027

windows.body: AI datacenter load (4.4% of US electricity heading to 7-12% by 2028) collides with a grid that takes 5+ years to interconnect anything. Gas turbines sold out to ~2029. The only dispatchable, carbon-acceptable, large-block answer at hyperscaler scale is nuclear — so the buyers with infinite money (Microsoft, Google, Amazon, Meta) started signing 20-year PPAs and the politics flipped in one cycle.

windows.title: Defense Tech Rearmament
windows.mechanism: Ukraine (2022) broke two consensuses at once: that interstate war was obsolete, and that $10M missiles beat $500 drones. Cost-curve inversion (cheap autonomous systems beating exquisite platforms) + NATO rearmament budgets + Silicon Valley's post-Maven allergy wearing off = a procurement regime shift that happens once in 40 years. The primes' cost-plus model cannot ship software-speed iterations; the gap is the window.
effects: Anduril ~$30B+ valuation; from CFIUS-pariah category to program-of-record wins in under a decade
Palantir rerated into a top-tier US company on defense-AI demand
European rearmament (Germany's Zeitenwende, ~2-3.5% GDP targets) opening the first new prime-scale market since the Cold War
Drone/counter-drone as the highest-velocity category: Ukraine iterates weekly; procurement systems built for decades cannot keep up
windows.decay_logic: Windows close via incumbency: today's insurgents (Anduril, Palantir) become tomorrow's primes and recreate the moats they attacked. Peace dividend reversal would slow it but the demographic-fiscal politics of NATO+Asia make budget reversal unlikely before 2030

entry_checklist.text: Does the product ship in months and iterate against live conflict feedback?
Is the cost-exchange ratio favorable (your unit cost vs what it destroys/defends)?
Program-of-record path identified, or selling to the valley-of-death between SBIR and production?

who_won: Ukrainian drone manufacturers (the live R&D lab)
Thiel's Founders Fund thesis, a decade early
who_died: Defense startups 2015-2020 that died in the procurement valley before the regime shift (the timing graveyard)
Primes' software ventures (organizationally unable)
windows.open_signal: Procurement reform bills still passing; insurgents still winning programs-of-record against primes; European budgets still rising. Watch: first $10B+ program won outright by a non-prime.

future_paths.path: Anduril IPOs as the first new prime in 60 years
future_paths.falsifier: No IPO filing and no program-of-record >$1B by end 2027

future_paths.path: Autonomous systems doctrine formalized; manned-platform budgets structurally decline
future_paths.falsifier: Next-gen manned fighter/carrier budgets grow in real terms through 2029

future_paths.path: The window closes by insurgent-becomes-incumbent consolidation
future_paths.falsifier: New defense startups still winning first contracts at 2024-25 rates in 2029

windows.body: Ukraine (2022) broke two consensuses at once: that interstate war was obsolete, and that $10M missiles beat $500 drones. Cost-curve inversion (cheap autonomous systems beating exquisite platforms) + NATO rearmament budgets + Silicon Valley's post-Maven allergy wearing off = a procurement regime shift that happens once in 40 years. The primes' cost-plus model cannot ship software-speed iterations; the gap is the window.

windows.title: Chip Reshoring / Friendshoring
windows.mechanism: CHIPS Act ($52B) + EU Chips Act + Japan's Rapidus + export-control regime = the first state-directed reorganization of semiconductor geography since the industry left the US in the 1980s. The driver is the atlas's most concentrated chokepoint: >90% of leading-edge logic within 110 miles of the Taiwan Strait. States are paying a structural inefficiency premium (~20-50% higher fab costs) as insurance — GDP-negative, resilience-positive, and a decade-long flow of capital to whoever builds, equips, and staffs fabs.
effects: TSMC Arizona: N4 yields at Taiwan parity (2025), $165B+ committed — the existence proof
Equipment makers (ASML, AMAT, TEL) selling into duplicated capacity — the certain winners regardless of which fabs succeed
Japan's Rapidus partnership with IBM/imec targeting 2nm — the boldest leapfrog attempt
Skilled-labor shortage revealed as the real constraint: fabs can be built faster than workforces
windows.decay_logic: Closes when duplicated capacity meets demand reality ~2030+: if AI demand disappoints, the subsidized fabs become stranded assets and the premium becomes politically indefensible. Also closes on success: once geographic risk is priced out, the insurance stops being bought

entry_checklist.text: Is the play in the certain layer (equipment, materials, construction, workforce) or the speculative layer (which fab wins)?
Does it survive a 30% demand disappointment?
Is the subsidy already committed or still political?

who_won: TSMC (subsidized diversification it would never have self-funded)
who_died: Intel as originally planned beneficiary (foundry stumbles consumed the thesis; rescue structure still unfolding, cf:md)
Wolfspeed and second-tier names that over-built into the subsidy narrative
windows.open_signal: Subsidies still being legislated; TSMC/Samsung still announcing US/EU/Japan expansions; export controls still tightening. Watch: Intel 18A external customer wins as the second-source reality test.

future_paths.path: US/Japan reach ~20% of leading-edge capacity by 2030 — insurance achieved at premium cost
future_paths.falsifier: Leading-edge ex-Taiwan share still <12% in 2029

future_paths.path: Intel foundry fails as second source; the duplication is all TSMC-owned and Taiwan leverage persists
future_paths.falsifier: Intel lands 2+ top-10 fabless customers on 18A/14A by 2027

future_paths.path: Demand disappointment strands subsidized capacity; political backlash against chips industrial policy
future_paths.falsifier: Fab utilization at new western sites >80% through 2029

windows.body: CHIPS Act ($52B) + EU Chips Act + Japan's Rapidus + export-control regime = the first state-directed reorganization of semiconductor geography since the industry left the US in the 1980s. The driver is the atlas's most concentrated chokepoint: >90% of leading-edge logic within 110 miles of the Taiwan Strait. States are paying a structural inefficiency premium (~20-50% higher fab costs) as insurance — GDP-negative, resilience-positive, and a decade-long flow of capital to whoever builds, equips, and staffs fabs.

windows.title: Sovereign AI Buildouts
windows.mechanism: States conclude frontier AI is a strategic capability they cannot rent from another jurisdiction. Gulf states recycle petrodollars into compute (MGX/Stargate, G42); the EU funds AI factories; Japan/India/Korea fund national champions. The structural feature: strategic buyers are price-insensitive — they don't mark compute to commercial returns, which extends the capex cycle beyond what market demand alone supports and creates a buyer-of-last-resort under GPU prices.
effects: Gulf gigawatt datacenter deals with US chip-access strings attached — compute diplomacy as the new arms sales
NVIDIA/AMD demand base diversifies beyond hyperscalers — the cycle-extension nobody models
Talent and model-weights residency requirements emerging — the regulatory fragmentation trade
Middle powers (UAE, Saudi, Singapore) buying relevance they could never build organically
windows.decay_logic: Forming → open as deals convert to energized datacenters 2026-27. Closes via either commoditization (open-weights models good enough that sovereignty is cheap) or disillusionment (national champions produce white elephants — the historical base rate for state tech champions is poor)

entry_checklist.text: Is the buyer's budget strategic (defense-adjacent) or commercial (must show returns)?
Does the play survive open-weights models reaching 90% of frontier capability?
Are chip-access export terms durable or one election away from reversal?

who_died: Pre-2024 national AI strategies that funded papers instead of compute (most of them)
windows.open_signal: FORMING: deals signed but datacenters not yet energized. Flips OPEN when first sovereign gigawatt cluster runs frontier training. Watch: whether open-weights progress makes the whole premise cheap before the concrete cures.

future_paths.path: Sovereign demand becomes a durable 15-20% of global AI capex — the cycle extender
future_paths.falsifier: Sovereign share of accelerator purchases <8% in 2027 tracking

future_paths.path: Open-weights commoditization makes sovereignty cheap; the buildouts become white elephants
future_paths.falsifier: Frontier-to-open capability gap widens through 2028

future_paths.path: Compute diplomacy formalizes into bloc structures (chip-access treaties mirroring arms control)
future_paths.falsifier: No multilateral compute-access framework by 2029

windows.body: States conclude frontier AI is a strategic capability they cannot rent from another jurisdiction. Gulf states recycle petrodollars into compute (MGX/Stargate, G42); the EU funds AI factories; Japan/India/Korea fund national champions. The structural feature: strategic buyers are price-insensitive — they don't mark compute to commercial returns, which extends the capex cycle beyond what market demand alone supports and creates a buyer-of-last-resort under GPU prices.

windows.title: GLP-1 / Metabolic Reset
windows.mechanism: The non-AI proof that the framework generalizes. A 30-year slow variable (Novo's patient GLP-1 research since the 1990s) crossed efficacy thresholds (semaglutide ~15%, tirzepatide ~20%+ weight loss) and collided with a population that is ~40% obese in the US. Result: the largest new drug market in history, Novo briefly Europe's most valuable company, Lilly approaching $1T — and second-order demand effects (food, alcohol, retail, insurance, airlines' fuel math) that markets are still mispricing.
effects: Novo/Lilly duopoly captured a market growing toward $100B+/yr (cf:md)
Food/beverage incumbents repricing on volume fears — the first demand-side shock to calories in history
Compounding pharmacies + gray market as the price-umbrella arbitrage (closing as supply normalizes)
Oral formulations + next-gen (triple agonists, muscle-sparing) extending the patent runway into the 2030s
windows.decay_logic: Patent cliffs (semaglutide ~2031-32) + oral generics + Chinese biosims close the pricing umbrella; the BEHAVIORAL window (population-scale appetite modification effects on adjacent industries) runs longer than the pharma-profit window

entry_checklist.text: Is the play on the duopoly, the supply chain (peptide manufacturing, auto-injectors), or the second-order demand shifts?
Does it survive 70% price deflation post-patent-cliff?
Is the adjacent-industry effect (food, apparel, ortho) actually showing in that company's volumes, or is it narrative?

who_won: Novo Nordisk (then gave back much of the rerating as supply/competition normalized — the round trip is itself the lesson)
Peptide CDMOs (the picks-and-shovels: Catalent acquired by Novo holdings)
who_died: Pfizer's oral candidates (discontinued — being late + second-rate in a duopoly market is fatal)
Weight Watchers (bankrupt 2025 — the behavioral-modification incumbent obsoleted by pharmacology)
windows.open_signal: Supply still constrained in cycles; new indications (sleep apnea, cardiovascular, kidney, alcohol use) still expanding the label; adjacent-industry volume effects still emerging in earnings calls. Watch: first generic/biosimilar approvals as the close-clock.

future_paths.path: GLP-1 class reaches >10% of US adults; second-order demand effects become measurable macro variables
future_paths.falsifier: US adult penetration stalls <5% on access/cost through 2028

future_paths.path: Muscle-sparing + oral next-gens make pharmacological body-composition control a permanent consumer category
future_paths.falsifier: Next-gen trials show no improvement over injectables / safety signals emerge

future_paths.path: The food-industry demand shock proves overhyped; volumes recover as adherence disappoints
future_paths.falsifier: Long-term adherence >50% at 24 months in real-world data

windows.body: The non-AI proof that the framework generalizes. A 30-year slow variable (Novo's patient GLP-1 research since the 1990s) crossed efficacy thresholds (semaglutide ~15%, tirzepatide ~20%+ weight loss) and collided with a population that is ~40% obese in the US. Result: the largest new drug market in history, Novo briefly Europe's most valuable company, Lilly approaching $1T — and second-order demand effects (food, alcohol, retail, insurance, airlines' fuel math) that markets are still mispricing.

windows.title: Stablecoin Payment Rails
windows.mechanism: GENIUS Act (2025) gave dollar-stablecoins a federal framework; Circle's IPO gave the category a public benchmark. The quiet compounder: settlement that bypasses correspondent banking (cross-border B2B, remittances, treasury ops) grows at internet speed inside a financial system that moves at SWIFT speed. The live wildcard: AI agents transacting autonomously need programmable, instant, low-cost money far more than humans do — agent commerce may be the demand shock nobody is positioned for.
effects: Stablecoin supply >$250B and growing; Tether among the largest holders of US Treasuries (cf:md)
Banks lobbying on deposit-flight risk — their cheapest funding source is what's actually contested
Visa/Mastercard/Stripe all building stablecoin settlement — incumbent absorption already underway
Dollarization-by-stablecoin in high-inflation economies (Argentina, Turkey, Nigeria) — monetary policy leakage as a feature
windows.decay_logic: Forming → open as regulated US issuance scales 2026-27. Closes via incumbent absorption (card networks + banks issue their own, capturing the rails) or via a depeg/issuer failure triggering restrictive re-regulation — the category's recurring near-death pattern (Terra 2022)

entry_checklist.text: Is the play on issuers (margin = short-term rates — a rate-cut casualty), infrastructure (wallets, compliance, orchestration), or the agent-commerce wildcard?
Does it survive the card networks shipping equivalent rails?
Counterparty: is the issuer's reserve attestation real?

who_won: Circle (the regulated-first bet paying off at IPO)
Tether (the offshore incumbent printing billions in float income)
who_died: Terra/UST (algorithmic stablecoin, $40B collapse 2022 — the design that didn't survive contact with reflexivity)
Facebook's Libra/Diem (right thesis, wrong messenger — killed by regulators 2019-22, the door GENIUS reopened for everyone else)
windows.open_signal: FORMING: framework live, bank/card-network entries announced but not scaled. Flips OPEN when monthly stablecoin settlement volume crosses card-network scale in any major corridor. Watch: agent-to-agent payment standards (x402 etc.) as the wildcard accelerant.

future_paths.path: Stablecoin rails capture >10% of cross-border B2B settlement
future_paths.falsifier: Cross-border stablecoin volume share <3% in 2028 measurements

future_paths.path: Agent commerce becomes the killer app; programmable money demand explodes with autonomous agents
future_paths.falsifier: Agent-initiated transaction volume immaterial (<$10B/yr) by 2028

future_paths.path: A major issuer failure triggers restrictive re-regulation and the window slams
future_paths.falsifier: Top-3 issuers pass full reserve audits through 2027

windows.body: GENIUS Act (2025) gave dollar-stablecoins a federal framework; Circle's IPO gave the category a public benchmark. The quiet compounder: settlement that bypasses correspondent banking (cross-border B2B, remittances, treasury ops) grows at internet speed inside a financial system that moves at SWIFT speed. The live wildcard: AI agents transacting autonomously need programmable, instant, low-cost money far more than humans do — agent commerce may be the demand shock nobody is positioned for.
